scope.area: dedicated request latency measurement under single-main-process PM2 fork mode
coverage: cold controller route measurement with tenant spawn correlation
warm-only repeated measurements for redirect, static, controller, and session routes
latency-only analysis separated from installer lifecycle noise

summary.highLevelVerdict: Under the current single-main-process fork topology, the large spread seen in the earlier lifecycle report is explained by cold-versus-warm mixing rather than unstable steady-state localhost performance. Warm redirect, static, controller, and session requests stayed in a tight 5.58 ms to 13.158 ms band, while the first controller request took 361.145 ms because it included tenant process launch and bootstrap.

runtimeTopology.sessionWarmMethod: One warmup request created and reused a cookie jar before the repeated `/session` sample set.

commands.warmSeries: For each route, issue one warmup request and then 20 repeated requests with `curl -sk --max-time 1`; reuse a cookie jar for `/session`.

coldRunProcedure: Start the runtime in single-instance fork mode.
Measure the first successful static route directly after startup without prior warmup.
Restart the runtime, then measure `/hello` as the first controller-backed request to isolate tenant cold start.
warmRunProcedure: Use the exact same local transport and host header for every route.
Issue one warmup request per route before collecting repeated samples.
Record 20 repeated successful samples per route with `curl --max-time 1`.
Reuse session cookies for the warm `/session` series so first-session setup is not mixed into the steady-state samples.
assumptions: The canonical measurement host for this report is `www.fakedomain.com`, because that is the scaffolded host created by setup.
Redirect timings were measured on the `302` response itself and did not follow the redirect target.
Local requests exceeding 1 second would be treated as timeouts and excluded as failures, but no request hit that threshold.

firstSuccessfulAfterRuntimeStart.tenantSpawnEvidence: No explicit tenant launch line was observed around the first static request in `pm2-out-0.log`.

explicitControllerColdStart.interpretation: The first controller request overlapped tenant launch, tenant bootstrap, privilege switch, tenant entrypoint load, and tenant-ready notification.

coldVsWarm.note: Fastest and most stable steady-state path because it returns a local `302` without tenant controller or session work.
Cold static stayed noticeably above warm static, but still far below the controller cold start.
This is the clearest proof that the earlier 300 ms class timings were dominated by tenant cold start instead of steady-state controller execution.
Highest warm median and widest warm spread in this run, which fits the extra session-routing and session-state work.

analysis.rootCauseOfEarlierSpread: The March 23, 2026 lifecycle report combined cold first-hit controller timing with warm redirect and warm static responses in one table. Under the isolated single-fork run, warm paths no longer show a wide 8 ms to 317 ms spread; the large number belongs specifically to tenant cold start.
steadyStateInterpretation: Warm redirect responses are consistently low and almost flat, with only 0.553 ms spread across 20 samples.
Warm static and warm controller timings sit in nearly the same band, which suggests the controller dispatch layer adds little steady-state overhead once the tenant is already live.
Warm session requests remain the most expensive steady-state class, but they still stayed between 9.406 ms and 13.158 ms and never approached the 1 second timeout threshold.
The remaining 8 ms to 10 ms warm floor is not raw in-process localhost speed, but it is consistent with local HTTPS plus manager, engine, and tenant process coordination.
analysis.acceptabilityDecision: The warm-path latency under the single-instance fork model looks acceptable for localhost testing. No new optimization tasklist was created from this run.
analysis.actionability: The previous mixed cold/warm interpretation should now be treated as resolved. Any future latency optimization should focus on tenant cold-start cost rather than the warm request path.

runtimeLogCorrelations: Main, manager, and engine reached `ready` in the single-fork topology before the cold route probes began.
The cold controller request aligned with `tenant_www.fakedomain.com` launch start at `2026-03-23T13:42:02.548Z` and tenant ready at `2026-03-23T13:42:02.877Z`.
The tenant process switched privileges to `e_tenant_www.fakedomain.com:root` and loaded `/var/opt/ehecatl/tenants/fakedomain.com/www/app/index.js` during that same window.
supportingEvidence.errorLogReview: The PM2 error log only recorded expected signal entries from the deliberate restart during measurement setup; no permission errors, request exceptions, or crash loops were observed.

requiredChecks.item: The active PM2 runtime shape is verified as `fork` with `instances: 1`
The local timing protocol uses `curl --max-time 1` consistently
Cold tenant spawn timing is measured separately from warm repeated timing
Redirect, static, controller, and session routes each have their own latency samples
At least one warm-only repeated route sample set is summarized with spread information
A dedicated `report/performance` artifact is written for request latency only
